1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechAcyclic.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/PresheafCech.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FreePresheafComplex.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechBridge.lean
8	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImagePresheaf.lean
9	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AbsoluteCohomology.lean
10	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechToCohomology.lean
11	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
12	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
13	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
14	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
15	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
16	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
17	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
18	
19	\section{Setup and motivation}
20	
21	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
22	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
23	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
24	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
25	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
26	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
27	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
28	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
29	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
30	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
31	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
32	
33	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
34	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
35	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
36	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
37	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
38	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
39	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
40	\[
41	  \begin{array}{ccc}
42	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
43	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
44	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
45	  \end{array}
46	\]
47	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
48	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
49	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
50	
51	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
52	
53	\begin{definition}
54	
55	\leanok
56	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
57	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
59	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
60	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
61	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
62	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
63	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
64	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
65	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
66	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
67	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
68	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
69	  % the unit ideal, see
70	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
71	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
72	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
73	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
74	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
75	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
76	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
77	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
78	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
79	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
80	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
81	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
82	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
83	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
84	  \[
85	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
86	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
87	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
88	  \]
89	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
90	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
91	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
92	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
93	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
94	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
95	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
96	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
97	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
98	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
99	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
100	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
101	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
102	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
103	\end{definition}
104	
105	\begin{definition}
106	
107	\leanok
108	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
109	  \label{def:cech_complex}
110	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
111	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
112	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
113	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
114	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
115	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
116	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
117	  % is identified with the complex
118	  % $$
119	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
120	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
121	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
122	  % \ldots
123	  % $$
124	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
125	  % \begin{equation}
126	  % \label{equation-extended}
127	  % 0 \to
128	  % M \to
129	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
130	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
131	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
132	  % \ldots
133	  % \end{equation}
134	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
135	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
136	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
137	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
138	  vanishing).}
139	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
140	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
141	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
142	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
143	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
144	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
145	  in degree \(p\) it is
146	  \[
147	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
148	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
149	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
150	  \]
151	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
152	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
153	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
154	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
155	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
156	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
157	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
158	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
159	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
160	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
161	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
162	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
163	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
164	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
165	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
166	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
167	\end{definition}
168	
169	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
170	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
171	
172	It is convenient to factor the construction of
173	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
174	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
175	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
176	
177	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
178	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
179	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
180	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
181	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
182	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
183	\[
184	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
185	    \;=\;
186	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
187	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
188	\]
189	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
190	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
191	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
192	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
193	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
194	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
195	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
196	
197	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
198	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
199	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
200	\[
201	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
202	  \qquad
203	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
204	\]
205	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
206	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
207	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
208	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
209	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
210	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
211	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
212	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
213	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
214	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
215	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
216	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
217	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
218	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
219	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
220	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
221	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
222	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
223	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
224	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
225	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
226	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
227	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
228	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
229	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
230	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
231	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
232	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
233	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
234	
235	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
236	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
237	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
238	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
239	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
240	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
241	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
242	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
243	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
244	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
245	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
246	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
247	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
248	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
249	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
250	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
251	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
252	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
253	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
254	
255	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
256	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
257	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
258	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
259	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
260	
261	\begin{definition}\leanok
262	[Arrow of an affine cover]
263	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
264	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
266	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
267	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
268	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
269	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
270	  apply.
271	\end{definition}
272	
273	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
274	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
276	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
277	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
278	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
279	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
280	  \[
281	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
282	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
283	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
284	  \]
285	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
286	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
287	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
288	\end{definition}
289	
290	\begin{definition}\leanok
291	[Push--pull object map]
292	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
294	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
295	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
296	  \[
297	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
298	  \]
299	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
300	\end{definition}
301	
302	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
303	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
305	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
306	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
307	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
308	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
309	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
310	  \[
311	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
312	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
313	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
314	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
315	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
316	      \;=\;
317	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
318	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
319	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
320	  \]
321	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
322	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
323	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
324	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
325	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
326	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
331	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
333	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
334	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
335	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{proof}
339	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
340	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
341	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
342	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
343	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
344	\end{proof}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}
347	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
348	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
350	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
351	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
352	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
353	  \[
354	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
355	  \]
356	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
357	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
358	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
359	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
360	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
365	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
366	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
367	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
368	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
369	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
370	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
371	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
372	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
373	\end{proof}
374	
375	\begin{lemma}
376	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
377	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
379	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
380	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
381	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
382	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
383	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
384	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
385	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
386	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
387	  \[
388	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
389	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
390	    \;=\;
391	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
392	  \]
393	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
394	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
395	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
396	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
397	\end{lemma}
398	
399	\begin{proof}
400	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
401	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
402	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
403	\end{proof}
404	
405	\begin{lemma}
406	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
407	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
409	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
410	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
411	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
412	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
413	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
414	  collapses to the transport-light form
415	  \[
416	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
417	      \;\circ\; e_2
418	    \;=\;
419	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
420	      \;\circ\; e_3,
421	  \]
422	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
423	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
424	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
425	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
426	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
427	  composition (pentagon) law.
428	\end{lemma}
429	
430	\begin{proof}
431	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
432	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
433	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
434	\end{proof}
435	
436	\begin{definition}
437	[Push--pull functor]
438	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
439	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
440	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
441	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
442	  \[
443	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
444	  \]
445	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
446	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
447	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
448	  laws established above: the identity law
449	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
450	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
451	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
452	\end{definition}
453	
454	\begin{definition}
455	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
456	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
458	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
459	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
460	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
461	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
462	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
463	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
464	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
465	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
466	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
467	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
468	\end{definition}
469	
470	\begin{definition}\leanok
471	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
472	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
474	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
475	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
476	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
477	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
478	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
479	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
480	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
481	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
482	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
483	\end{definition}

485	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
486	
487	\begin{definition}
488	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
489	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
491	  \mathlibok
492	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
493	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
494	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
495	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
496	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
497	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
498	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
499	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
500	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
501	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
502	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
503	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
504	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
505	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
506	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
507	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
508	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
512	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
513	
514	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
515	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
516	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
517	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
518	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
519	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
520	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
521	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
522	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
523	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
524	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
525	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
526	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
527	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
528	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
529	
530	\begin{definition}
531	
532	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
533	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
535	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
536	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
537	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
538	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
539	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
540	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
541	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
542	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
543	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
544	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
545	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
546	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
547	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
548	  % as an $R_f$-module.
549	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
550	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
551	  % are the maps
552	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
553	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
554	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
555	  items (4)--(5).}
556	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
557	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
558	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
559	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
560	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
561	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
562	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
563	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
564	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
565	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
566	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
567	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
568	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
569	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
570	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
571	
572	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
573	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
574	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
575	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
576	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
577	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
578	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
579	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
580	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
581	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
582	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
583	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
584	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
585	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
586	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
587	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
588	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
589	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
590	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
591	
592	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
593	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
594	  intersection identity
595	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
596	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
597	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
598	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
599	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
600	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
601	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
602	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
603	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
604	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
605	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
606	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
607	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
608	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
609	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
610	
611	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
612	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
613	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
614	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
615	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
616	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
617	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
618	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
619	  restriction map through these comparisons.
620	\end{definition}
621	
622	\begin{lemma}
623	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
624	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
625	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
626	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
627	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
628	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
629	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,
630	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison,
631	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
632	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
633	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
634	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
635	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,

648	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact,
649	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
650	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
651	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
652	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
653	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
654	  % L58--67).
655	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
656	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
657	  % is identified with the complex
658	  % $$
659	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
660	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
661	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
662	  % \ldots
663	  % $$"
664	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
665	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
666	  complex of localisations).}
667	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
668	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
669	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
670	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
671	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
672	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
673	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
674	  \[
675	    D^\bullet : \quad
676	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
677	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
678	    \cdots,
679	    \qquad
680	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
681	  \]
682	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
683	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
684	  \[
685	    1 \leq p
686	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
687	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
688	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
689	  \]
690	  which is the form the affine vanishing
691	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
692	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
693	  homomorphisms,
694	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
695	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
696	
697	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
698	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
699	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
700	  product}
701	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
702	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
703	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
704	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
705	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
706	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
707	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
708	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
709	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
710	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
711	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
712	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
713	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
714	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
715	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
716	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
717	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
718	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
719	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
720	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
721	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
722	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
723	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
724	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
725	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
726	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
727	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
728	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
729	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
730	\end{lemma}
731	
732	\begin{proof}
733	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
734	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
735	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
736	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
737	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
738	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
739	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
740	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
741	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
742	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
743	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
744	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
745	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
746	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
747	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
748	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
749	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
750	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
751	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
752	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
753	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
754	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
755	  element-level product equivalence
756	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
757	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
758	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
759	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
760	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
761	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
762	
763	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
764	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
765	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
766	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
767	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
768	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
769	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
770	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
771	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
772	  being proved.
773	\end{proof}

775	\begin{lemma}
776	
777	[Element-level product equivalence for the section {\v C}ech terms]
778	  \label{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
779	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv,
780	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv_apply,
781	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdAddEquiv}
782	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
783	  Let \(\{F_\sigma\}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}\) be the finite family of
784	  abelian groups \(F_\sigma = \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) appearing in degree \(p\) of
785	  the section {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}. There is
786	  an equivalence of underlying types
787	  \[
788	    \operatorname{ToType}\Bigl(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\Bigr)
789	    \;\simeq\;
790	    \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma),
791	  \]
792	  between the elements of the categorical product \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\) in
793	  \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the dependent functions \(\sigma \mapsto\) (element of
794	  \(F_\sigma\)). It is supplied by the Mathlib concrete-limit comparison
795	  \(\operatorname{CategoryTheory.Limits.Concrete.productEquiv}\), which holds because the
796	  forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete
797	  product (\(\operatorname{PreservesLimit}(\operatorname{Discrete.functor} F)\)). Under
798	  this equivalence each coordinate
799	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)))\) is identified with the away
800	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{dCoeff}\) of
801	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} by the
802	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) uniqueness of
803	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} (both
804	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and \(M_{s_\sigma}\) localise \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\), so
805	  the comparison is the canonical isomorphism). This is the move between the
806	  categorical product of \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the per-\(\sigma\) localised modules.
807	  Its packaging as an additive isomorphism --- the degreewise vertical map of the
808	  ladder transport in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact} --- is recorded as
809	  \(\operatorname{sectionProdAddEquiv}\), the \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) underlying
810	  the coordinatewise comparison.
811	  The coordinate projection of the equivalence is recorded separately as the
812	  definitional restatement
813	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\): the \(\sigma\)-component of the
814	  image of \(y\) under the equivalence is the underlying group map of the categorical
815	  projection \(\pi_\sigma\) applied to \(y\).
816	\end{lemma}
817	
818	\begin{proof}
819	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
820	  The type-level equivalence is \(\operatorname{Concrete.productEquiv}\) applied to the
821	  family \(\sigma \mapsto F_\sigma\); its hypothesis is that
822	  \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete-diagram limit, which is
823	  the standard instance for \(\mathrm{Ab}\). The per-coordinate identification
824	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))) \cong M_{s_\sigma}\) is the
825	  uniqueness clause of \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\): by
826	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} the restriction map
827	  \(M \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and the localisation unit
828	  \(M \to M_{s_\sigma}\) are both localisations of \(M\) at
829	  \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\)
830	  (\(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\)), hence canonically isomorphic over
831	  \(M\). Assembling the coordinatewise isomorphisms after the product equivalence gives
832	  the stated identification.
833	\end{proof}

835	\begin{lemma}
836	[Abstract cosimplicial unfold of the section {\v C}ech differential]
837	  \label{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}
838	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_objD_apply,
839	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechFaceRestr}
840	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
841	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
842	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open cover. For a multi-index
843	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(q+2) \to I\) and a face index
844	  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
845	  \[
846	    \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) :
847	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)
848	      \;\longrightarrow\;
849	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr)
850	  \]
851	  for the \(i\)-th presheaf face restriction --- the value of \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
852	  inclusion of opens
853	  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)
854	  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
855	  read through the product equivalence
856	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\) of
857	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
858	  degree-\(q\) differential
859	  \(\operatorname{objD} q\) of the alternating-coface-map complex
860	  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}
861	  (\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\,\mathcal{U}\,\mathcal{F})\) is the alternating
862	  sum of the face restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates:
863	  \[
864	    \bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q+1}\,(\operatorname{objD} q\, t)\bigr)_\sigma
865	      \;=\;
866	      \sum_{i = 0}^{q+1} (-1)^i \,
867	        \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
868	          \Bigl(\bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q}\, t\bigr)_{\sigma \circ d_i}\Bigr).
869	  \]
870	  This is \emph{purely cosimplicial}: it records the shape of the section {\v C}ech
871	  differential as an alternating sum of presheaf restrictions, with \emph{no}
872	  sheaf-theoretic or localisation identification of those restrictions. It is the
873	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
874	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge
875	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} is supplied.
876	\end{lemma}
877	
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
880	  Unfold the degree-\(q\) map of \(\operatorname{alternatingCofaceMapComplex}\) on
881	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\): by construction \(\operatorname{objD} q\)
882	  is the alternating sum \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, d^i\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, each
883	  coface \(d^i\) being the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component
884	  projects to the coordinate \(\sigma \circ d_i\) and post-composes with the presheaf
885	  restriction \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\). Reading the
886	  \(\sigma\)-coordinate through the product equivalence (the coordinate-projection
887	  restatement \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\)) commutes the projection
888	  past the categorical sum: applying the sum of \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-morphisms to an element
889	  distributes over the sum (the finite-sum-application identity
890	  \(\operatorname{ab\_hom\_finsetSum\_apply}\)), and each summand becomes
891	  \((-1)^i\) times the face restriction of the \((\sigma \circ d_i)\)-coordinate. The
892	  per-coordinate projection of the cosimplicial coface is the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\)
893	  computation \(\operatorname{Pi.lift\_π}\), which identifies the projected coface with
894	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) precomposed with the projection to
895	  \(\sigma \circ d_i\). Assembling the signs yields the stated alternating sum.
896	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}
58

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair,
273	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ₁, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.f_comp_horseshoeβ₁,
274	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.g_comp_horseshoeH,
275	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero,
276	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero_hf, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_zero,
277	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_fst,
278	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_snd, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0,
279	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_τZero,
280	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero}
281	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
282	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
283	  \[
284	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
285	  \]
286	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
287	  identity
288	  \[
289	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
290	  \]
291	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
292	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
293	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
294	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
295	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
296	\end{lemma}
297	
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
300	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
301	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
302	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
303	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
304	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
305	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
306	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
307	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
308	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
309	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
310	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
311	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
313	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
314	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
315	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
316	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
317	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
318	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
319	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
320	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
322	\end{proof}
323	
324	\begin{lemma}\leanok
325	[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
326	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
327	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_comp}
328	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
329	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
330	  differential degreewise by the matrix
331	  \[
332	    d_B^n \;=\;
333	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
334	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
335	  \]
336	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
337	  cochain complex.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
342	  \leanok
343	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
344	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
345	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
346	  \[
347	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
348	  \]
349	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
350	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
351	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
352	\end{proof}
353	
354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ,
360	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_X, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_d,
361	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_fst, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_snd,
362	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd}
363	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
364	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
365	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
366	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
367	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
368	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
369	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
370	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
371	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
372	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
373	\end{lemma}
374	
375	\begin{proof}
376	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
377	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
378	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
379	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
380	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
381	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
382	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
383	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
384	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
385	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
386	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
387	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
388	\end{proof}
