390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
391	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
392	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
393	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
394	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}

428	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
429	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
430	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,
431	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₁₂,
432	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₂₃, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₁,
433	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,
434	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.quasiIso_horseshoeι, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.single₀_hom_ext}
435	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
436	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
445	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes
450	  \[
451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]
454	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
458	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
459	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
460	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
461	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
462	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
463	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
464	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
465	\end{proof}

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok

683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}

693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere

702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)

717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}

813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}

873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection

897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

972	  (with \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{phi}\)) as \(\operatorname{phi\_naturality}\).
973	  Substituting (ii) into (i) and summing with signs
974	  identifies the section {\v C}ech differential with \(\operatorname{dDiff}\).
975	
976	  The isomorphism \(\varphi_\sigma\) is obtained by matching the two descriptions of
977	  the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(s_\sigma)\): the cosimplicial functor
978	  provides one description and the localisation property of
979	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} provides the other. Their agreement,
980	  together with the naturality square, is the content of this bridge.
981	\end{proof}

983	\begin{lemma}
984	[Abelian-group exactness criterion for the section {\v C}ech complex]
985	  \label{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
986	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechAbExact,
987	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_isZero_homology_of_objD_exact,
988	    AlgebraicGeometry.ab_hom_finsetSum_apply,
989	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionToModuleAddEquiv,

993	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_coface_match,
994	    lem:section_cech_module_exact}
995	  For \(1 \leq p\) the homology of the section {\v C}ech complex
996	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
997	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} vanishes,
998	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
999	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\). The argument splits into a homological
1000	  precursor and an unconditional ladder transport.
1001	  \begin{itemize}
1002	    \item The precursor \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\)
1003	      is pure homological algebra: \emph{given}
1004	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of two
1005	      consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
1006	      \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\), the homology of the section complex at degree
1007	      \(q+1\) is zero. It chains
1008	      \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\),
1009	      \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.exactAt\_iff'}\) (to expose the short complex
1010	      around the node), and the abelian-group exactness criterion
1011	      \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff\_function\_exact}\) (which states that
1012	      a short complex of abelian groups is exact iff for every \(x_2\) with
1013	      \(g(x_2) = 0\) there is \(x_1\) with \(f(x_1) = x_2\)).
1014	    \item The unconditional \(\operatorname{sectionCechAbExact}\) discharges that
1015	      hypothesis by transporting the already-proved \(R\)-module exactness
1016	      \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
1017	      Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the degreewise additive
1018	      isomorphism, using the ladder-transport lemma
1019	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\).
1020	  \end{itemize}
1021	\end{lemma}
1022	
1023	\begin{proof}
1024	  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, lem:section_cech_product_equiv,
1025	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1026	  Fix \(p = q + 1 \geq 1\). By the precursor
1027	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\) it suffices to
1028	  exhibit \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of the
1029	  two consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
1030	  \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\) of the section complex.
1031	
1032	  This exactness is obtained by the ladder transport
1033	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\). The vertical maps
1034	  of the ladder are the degreewise additive isomorphisms
1035	  \(e_p = \prod_\sigma \varphi_\sigma \circ \operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_p\):
1036	  the product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} followed by the
1037	  coordinatewise comparison \(\varphi_\sigma\) identifying each section group with the
1038	  localised module \(M_{s_\sigma}\). This composite vertical map is the additive
1039	  equivalence \(\operatorname{sectionToModuleAddEquiv}\), whose \(\sigma\)-component is
1040	  \(\operatorname{sectionToModuleAddEquiv\_apply}\) (the forward coordinate formula) and
1041	  whose inverse-direction coordinate is \(\operatorname{sectionProdEquiv\_symm\_apply}\). The two commuting squares of the ladder are the
1042	  coface match: the abstract unfold of the differential as an alternating sum of face
1043	  restrictions (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}, the decl
1044	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_objD\_apply}\)) together with the per-coordinate
1045	  identification of each face restriction with the localisation coface
1046	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}) shows
1047	  \(e_{p+1} \circ \operatorname{objD} p = \operatorname{dDiff}^{\,p} \circ e_p\). The
1048	  horizontal exactness fed into the transport is the \(R\)-module exactness
1049	  \(H = \operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
1050	  Transporting \(H\) across the additive ladder yields the desired
1051	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the \(\operatorname{objD}\) maps, discharging the
1052	  hypothesis of the precursor.
1053	\end{proof}

1056	[The un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\)]
1057	  \label{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1058	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dCoeff,
1059	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dCoface,

1080	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.smul_pow_cancel}
1081	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
1082	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
1083	  the global sections of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
1084	  R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) a spanning
1085	  family of the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) =
1086	  \top\) (Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}). The \emph{un-localised section
1087	  {\v C}ech module complex} \(D^\bullet\) is the cochain complex of \(R\)-modules
1088	  whose degree-\(p\) term is the product over multi-indices
1089	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of the away localisations
1090	  \[
1091	    D^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} M_{s_\sigma},
1092	    \qquad s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
1093	    \qquad M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}
1094	      \bigl(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\bigr)\, M,
1095	  \]
1096	  with differential \(d^p : D^p \to D^{p+1}\) the alternating sum
1097	  \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j\, \delta^j\) of the coface comparison maps \(\delta^j\), each of
1098	  which is the canonical away-localisation comparison
1099	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) sending a fraction to its image under
1100	  the further localisation at the omitted factor. The single-coefficient comparison
1101	  map and its localisation property are recorded by the \(\mathtt{dCoface}\) and
1102	  \(\mathtt{dCoeff}\) data and the differential by \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) (with action
1103	  \(\mathtt{dDiff\_apply}\)); the auxiliary \(\mathtt{spanIdx}\)/\(\mathtt{spanIdx\_spec}\)
1104	  select a witnessing index of the spanning relation.
1105	
1106	  The complex \(D^\bullet\) is tied to the section {\v C}ech complex of
1107	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three
1108	  comparison families. First, the maps \(\mathtt{dToCech}\) identify each term
1109	  \(D^p\) with the degree-\(p\) section group
1110	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) of
1111	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
1112	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
1113	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side
1114	  cofaces are \(\mathtt{cechCofaceLin}\) with action \(\mathtt{cechCoface\_apply}\).
1115	  Second, localising \(D^\bullet\) at a spanning element \(s_r\) reproduces the
1116	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1117	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
1118	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial
1119	  differential, and its exactness is recorded as \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\). The
1120	  localisation comparison maps \(\mathtt{fLoc}\) (action \(\mathtt{fLoc\_apply}\))
1121	  exhibit each localised term as a localised module
1122	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
1123	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).
1124	  The key algebraic fact underlying all three families is the localisation
1125	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\): localising the away module \(M_{s_\sigma}\)
1126	  further at \(s_r\) gives \(M_{s_r s_\sigma}\). This is the keystone
1127	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\) (the composite of two away
1128	  localisations is again the away localisation at the product), with the cancellation
1129	  bookkeeping \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.Inverts.smul\_pow\_cancel}\). Thus localising
1130	  \(D^\bullet\) at \(s_r\) (an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.Away}\)) reproduces
1131	  the localised Čech complex of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, and
1132	  exactness of \(D^\bullet\) can be checked one localisation at a time.
1133	\end{definition}

1135	\begin{lemma}
1136	
1137	\leanok
1138	[Positive-degree exactness of the section {\v C}ech module complex]
1139	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact}
1141	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1142	  With the notation of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, the
1143	  un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\) is exact in positive
1144	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1145	  \[
1146	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(d^{p+1} : D^{p+1} \to D^{p+2},\;
1147	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).
1148	  \]
1149	  This is step~(a) of the affine vanishing: the node-by-node exactness of the
1150	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section
1151	  {\v C}ech complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, step~(c)) is read off.
1152	\end{lemma}
1153	
1154	\begin{proof}
1155	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1156	  \leanok
1157	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1158	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1159	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1160	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1161	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1162	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1163	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1164	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1165	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1166	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1167	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that
1168	  localised differential is \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\), which is in turn the
1169	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial exactness transported along
1170	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\); the contracting homotopy at the fixed index
1171	  \(r\) (where \(s_r\) is invertible) furnishes \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\),
1172	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the
1173	  spanning family yields the un-localised exactness.
1174	\end{proof}

1323	  (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) is defined globally on that localisation and
1324	  furnishes a contracting homotopy of the localised complex: the unit, shift, and
1325	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\) (\(c \circ \delta_0 =
1326	  \operatorname{id}\)), \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (\(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)),
1327	  and \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) hold for the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\). The node is
1328	  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,
1329	  which packages the identity \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) into the
1330	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that
1331	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}\) consumes. The constant- and
1332	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial cores and the spanning-family certifier are
1333	  already in place; the section and differential identifications of
1334	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and the homology--exactness reduction of
1335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} are the bridge that connects them to the
1336	  abstract section complex.
1337	
1338	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
1339	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
1340	  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
1341	  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
1342	  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
1343	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
1344	  is used instead.
1345	\end{proof}

1347	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1348	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1349	
1350	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1351	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1352	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1353	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1354	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1355	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1356	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1357	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1358	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1359	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1360	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1361	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1362	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1363	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1364	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1365	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1366	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1367	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1368	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1369	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1370	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1371	
1372	\begin{definition}
1373	
1374	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
1375	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
1377	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,
1378	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,
1379	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,
1380	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen_comp_le,
1381	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial,
1382	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex_X,
1383	    AlgebraicGeometry.sigma_ι_eqToHom_transport}
1384	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1385	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1386	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1387	  % L1095--1135).
1388	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1389	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1390	  % $$
1391	  % \ldots
1392	  % \to
1393	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1394	  % \to
1395	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1396	  % \to
1397	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1398	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1399	  % $$
1400	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1401	  % and where the map
1402	  % $$
1403	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1404	  % \longrightarrow
1405	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1406	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1407	  % $$
1408	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1409	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1410	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1411	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1412	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1413	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1414	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1415	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1416	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1417	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1418	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1419	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1420	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1421	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1422	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1423	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1424	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1425	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1426	  \[
1427	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1428	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1429	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1430	  \]
1431	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1432	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1433	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1434	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1435	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1436	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1437	  \[
1438	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1439	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1440	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1441	  \]
1442	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1443	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1444	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1445	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1446	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1447	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1448	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1449	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1450	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1451	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1452	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1453	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1454	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1455	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1456	
1457	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1458	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1459	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1460	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1461	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1462	\end{definition}

1473	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L879--910).
1474	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Set
1475	  % $$
1476	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1477	  % =
1478	  % \prod\nolimits_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p + 1}}
1479	  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1480	  % $$
1481	  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
1482	  % $$
1483	  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1484	  % \longrightarrow
1485	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p + 1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1486	  % $$
1487	  % by the formula
1488	  % $$
1489	  % d(s)_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}
1490	  % =
1491	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^{p + 1}
1492	  % (-1)^j
1493	  % s_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}|_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1494	  % $$
1495	  % It is straightforward to see that $d \circ d = 0$."
1496	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1497	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1498	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1499	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1500	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1501	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1502	  \[
1503	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1504	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1505	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1506	  \]
1507	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1508	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1509	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1510	  \[
1511	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1512	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1513	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1514	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1515	  \]
1516	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1517	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1518	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1519	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1520	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1521	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1522	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1523	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1524	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1525	\end{definition}

1594	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1595	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1596	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1597	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1598	  \[
1599	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1600	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1601	    \bigr)
1602	    \;\cong\;
1603	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1604	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1605	    \;\cong\;
1606	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1607	  \]
1608	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1609	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1610	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1611	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1612	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1613	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1614	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1615	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1616	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1617	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1618	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1619	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1620	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1621	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1622	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1623	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1624	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1625	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1626	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1627	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1628	\end{proof}

1664	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term

1666	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the

1671	\begin{lemma}
1672	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1673	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1674	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1675	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1676	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1677	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1678	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1679	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1680	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1681	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1682	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1683	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1684	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1685	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1686	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1687	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1688	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1689	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1690	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1691	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1692	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1693	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1694	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1695	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1696	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1697	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1698	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1699	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1700	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1701	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1702	\end{lemma}
1703	
1704	\begin{proof}
1705	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1706	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1707	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1708	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1709	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1710	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1711	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1712	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1713	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1714	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1715	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1716	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1717	  quasi-isomorphism.
1718	\end{proof}

1790	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether

1792	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1793	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1794	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1795	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1796	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1797	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in

# Strategy

## Goal

Prove `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (`lem:cech_computes_cohomology`),
the protected, frozen-signature target: for `f : X ⟶ S` separated and quasi-compact, `F`
quasi-coherent, `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`, an isomorphism in the weak existence
form `Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F)` (under
`[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`), with `higherDirectImage f i F =
((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the cone, zero
project axioms, kernel-only axioms.

## Routes

### Route A — acyclic-resolution comparison (CHOSEN)
The augmented Čech complex `0 → F → C⁰ → C¹ → ⋯` on `X` (`Cᵖ = ∏ (j_s)_*(F|_{U_s})`) is
(i) a resolution of `F` and (ii) termwise `(pushforward f)`-acyclic. The P4 abstract lemma
gives `Hⁱ(f_* C•) ≅ Rⁱf_* F` directly — ONE abstract lemma, NO spectral sequences. Acyclicity
input (ii) reduces to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(affine, qcoh)=0`, supplied by the P3b bridge.

### Route B — two spectral sequences (REJECTED, fallback only)
Both absent from Mathlib (multi-thousand-LOC), and Leray needs quasi-coherence of `R^q f_* F`.
Rests on the same `injective_cech_acyclic` brick as A — rejecting B does not escape it.

### The acyclicity bridge (torsor-free, load-bearing)
Route A's acyclicity reduces to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(Spec A, qcoh)=0` (02KG). The honest
non-circular route is the minimal Čech↔derived bridge: (1) `injective_cech_acyclic`, (2)
`ses_cech_h1`, (3) the dimension-shift `cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis` (01EO) consuming the
standard-cover Čech vanishing of P3 as its condition (3). All three bricks of (1)+(2) are done;
01EO is the next target. This breaks the circular regress: P3 produces standard-cover Čech
vanishing; the bridge lifts it to affine sheaf vanishing without ever using affine vanishing.

### Absolute cohomology realization — Ext of the corepresenting object (Form B)
`H^p(U, F) := Ext^p_{X.Modules}(jShriekOU U, F)` where `jShriekOU U := sheafify(free(yoneda U))`
is the corepresenting object of `F ↦ Γ(U,F) = F(U)` (`= j_!O_U` up to iso), built from the
already-shipped `freeYonedaHomEquiv` + `sheafificationAdjunction` (~50–80 LOC). The three facts
01EO consumes are then off-the-shelf with the SES staying in `X.Modules`: injective vanishing
`Ext.eq_zero_of_injective` (injective `I` as the 2nd arg — no restriction taken), covariant LES
`Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁/₂/₃` at fixed 1st arg `jShriekOU U`, and `H⁰ ≅ Γ(U,F)` via the
corepresentability chain. The discarded alternatives: Form A `Ext(O_U, F|_U)` needs
restriction-preserves-injectives (≈ a 200–500 LOC `j_!` functor); `Functor.rightDerived` has
free injective vanishing but no packaged LES; `Sheaf.H` is absent. Reversal signal: Ext
universe/smallness pain over `SheafOfModules` → fall back to Route γ (Čech colimit), never `Sheaf.H`.

1913	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1914	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1915	  % $$"
1916	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1917	  (the evaluated complex \(K^{ext}_\bullet(W)\) and its differential).}
1918	  Fix an open \(V\) with \(I_1(V) = \{\, i : V \leq U_i \,\} \neq \varnothing\), and
1919	  let \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) be the single coefficient module
1920	  (\(\operatorname{coverSectionModule}\)). The \emph{engine chain complex}
1921	  \(C_\bullet = \operatorname{cechEngineComplex}\, \mathcal{U}\, V\) is the chain
1922	  complex of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-modules with
1923	  \[
1924	    C_p \;=\; \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1925	    \qquad (\operatorname{cechEngineX}),
1926	  \]
1927	  the coproduct taken in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(\mathcal{O}_X(V))\), and with
1928	  differential \(d = \operatorname{cechEngineD}\) given on the injection
1929	  \(\iota_\sigma\) of the index \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)\) by the
1930	  alternating sum of index-drop reindexings,
1931	  \[
1932	    d \circ \iota_\sigma
1933	      \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{p} (-1)^i\, \iota_{\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i}
1934	    \qquad (\operatorname{cechEngineD\_\iota}),
1935	  \]
1936	  i.e.\ the chain transpose of the index-\emph{raise} differential
1937	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) of
1938	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}. Then:
1939	  \begin{itemize}
1940	    \item \(d \circ d = 0\) (\(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_comp}\)), so the family
1941	      assembles into the chain complex \(C_\bullet = \operatorname{ChainComplex.of}\)
1942	      (\(\operatorname{cechEngineComplex}\)).
1943	    \item The \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} map
1944	      \(s \mapsto \iota_{\operatorname{Fin.cons}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, \sigma}\)
1945	      (\(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\), with its injection computation
1946	      \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend\_\iota}\)) is a contracting homotopy in
1947	      positive degree: \(s \circ d + d \circ s = \mathrm{id}\)
1948	      (\(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend\_spec}\)).
1949	    \item Hence \(C_\bullet\) is exact in positive degree,
1950	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(\operatorname{cechEngineD}(n+1),
1951	      \operatorname{cechEngineD} n)\) (\(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_exact}\)).
1952	  \end{itemize}
1953	  Four object-half bricks realise the degreewise identification
1954	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong C_p\) consumed by
1955	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}: evaluation commutes with the coproduct;
1956	  the non-surviving summands (those \(\sigma\) with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\)) drop to the
1957	  zero object (\(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
1958	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalEngine\_X}\)); and the surviving index type is reindexed
1959	  by the equivalence
1960	  \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv} : (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)) \simeq
1961	  \{\, \sigma \;//\; V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma \,\}\),
1962	  where the membership criterion
1963	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma
1964	  \iff \forall k,\ V \leq U_{\sigma(k)}\) is
1965	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\).
1966	\end{lemma}
1967	
1968	\begin{proof}
1969	  \uses{lem:free_cech_engine}
1970	  The complex \(C_\bullet\) is the chain/coproduct dual of the constant-coefficient
1971	  core of Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}. The differential
1972	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) is defined on coproduct injections by the
1973	  alternating-sign index-drop \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove}
1974	  i\); both \(d \circ d = 0\) and the contracting identity
1975	  \(s \circ d + d \circ s = \mathrm{id}\) are proved by extending along the
1976	  injections with \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) and reducing each
1977	  injection-component to the corresponding combinatorial cancellation of
1978	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential\_comp}\) respectively
1979	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\) (the prepend
1980	  \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\) is the coproduct dual of
1981	  \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\)). Positive-degree exactness
1982	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_exact}\) reads off the contracting identity. The four
1983	  object-half bricks are immediate: evaluation preserves \(\coprod\) and the surviving
1984	  summands are picked out by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) under the criterion
1985	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\), the rest being zero objects
1986	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\)).
1987	\end{proof}

2018	  % is given by

2026	  Fix an open \(V\) with \(I_1(V) = \{\, i : V \leq U_i \,\} \neq \varnothing\). There
2027	  is an isomorphism of chain complexes of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-modules
2028	  \[
2029	    \bigl((\operatorname{eval} V).\operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\bigr)
2030	      \bigl(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex} \mathcal{U}\bigr)
2031	    \;\cong\;
2032	    C_\bullet,
2033	    \qquad

2035	  \]

2037	  differential of \(C_\bullet\) is the constant-coefficient {\v C}ech differential
2038	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) on the index set \(J = I_1(V)\)
2039	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}). The isomorphism is \emph{degreewise} and
2040	  \emph{intertwines the evaluated alternating-face differential with}
2041	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\). It is the chain/coproduct dual of the section-side
2042	  cochain identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}; isolating it
2043	  here gives the empty and nonempty cases (Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}
2044	  and~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_nonempty}) a common, ready degreewise model.

2046	
2047	\begin{proof}
2048	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2049	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
2050	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
2051	  coproduct,
2052	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
2053	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
2054	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
2055	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
2056	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
2057	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
2058	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
2059	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
2060	  C_p\).
2061	
2062	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
2063	  applied to the alternating face sum
2064	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
2065	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
2066	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
2067	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
2068	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
2069	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
2070	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
2071	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
2072	  identifies the evaluated differential with
2073	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
2074	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
2075	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
2076	
2077	  \emph{The three layers commuted past.} The degreewise iso is the \(3\)-fold
2078	  composite
2079	  \[
2080	    (\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p)
2081	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\;}
2082	      \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0}
2083	        (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))
2084	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\;}
2085	      \coprod_{V \leq U_\sigma} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
2086	      \xrightarrow{\;(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \ldots)^{-1}\;}
2087	      C_p,
2088	  \]
2089	  whose layers are: (1) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) = the comparison
2090	  \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\) expressing that evaluation at
2091	  \(V\) commutes with the coproduct \(\coprod\); (2) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2092	  which kills the summands with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) (these evaluate to zero objects
2093	  by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\)); (3)
2094	  \((\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \operatorname{survivingEquiv}\
2095	  (\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\, \ldots))^{-1}\), which reindexes the
2096	  surviving summands by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) of
2097	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} and identifies each evaluated summand with the
2098	  single coefficient \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) via \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\).
2099	
2100	  \emph{Naturality past each layer.} The reindex \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) is
2101	  natural in the face map \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\):
2102	  a surviving index \(\sigma\) (i.e.\ \(V \leq U_\sigma\), equivalently
2103	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma\) by
2104	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\)) is carried to a surviving
2105	  \(\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (still \(V \leq U_{\sigma \circ
2106	  \operatorname{succAbove}\, i}\), since dropping a coordinate only weakens the meet),
2107	  so the drop-zeros layer (2) and the whisker layer (3) commute with every face. On a
2108	  surviving summand the evaluated transition \(\operatorname{freeYoneda}.\mathrm{map}
2109	  (\operatorname{homOfLE}\, \ldots)\) collapses to the identity under
2110	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); hence each face \(\delta_i\) matches the
2111	  engine reindexing \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\)
2112	  summand-for-summand, and summing with the \(\operatorname{objD}\) signs \((-1)^i\)
2113	  identifies the evaluated alternating-face differential with
2114	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) (\(= \) the chain transpose of
2115	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\)).
2116	
2117	  \emph{The genuine difficulty: variance.} The free \emph{chain} differential
2118	  \emph{lowers} \(\mathrm{Fin}\)-arity (\(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ
2119	  \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\), faces), whereas
2120	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) \emph{raises} it (it inserts an
2121	  index, summing over \(i \in I_1\)). The two are reconciled because on the finite
2122	  biproduct \(\coprod = \prod\) the insertion/deletion adjunction makes the index-drop
2123	  chain differential the transpose of the index-raise cochain differential; this
2124	  transpose is precisely what \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) of
2125	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} packages, and verifying the commutation amounts to
2126	  the injectionwise check \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) outlined above.
2127	\end{proof}

2146	  \(I_1(V) = \varnothing\), i.e.\ \(V\) is contained in no cover member \(U_i\). Then
2147	  the evaluated augmented complex is identically zero: every degree-\(p\) term
2148	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) is the empty

2151	  evaluation at \(V\) of \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\) is a morphism of zero
2152	  complexes, which is a quasi-isomorphism (both sides have vanishing homology). The Lean
2153	  bricks are \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (each evaluated source

2158	  augmentation target is zero), and the generic
2159	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_homology\_of\_isZero\_X}\) (a short complex with zero middle
2160	  term has vanishing homology), which together force every
2161	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) to be a map of zero objects.

2165	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
2166	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} each evaluated term is
2167	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\); when

2170	  morphism whose source and target complexes are both zero (so all their homology

2263	  prepend homotopy \(h\) satisfies the contracting identity
2264	  \[
2265	    d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}
2266	  \]
2267	  on the augmented evaluated complex
2268	  \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(V) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(V) \to
2269	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) \to 0\) (including the degree-\(0\)/degree-\((-1)\)
2270	  augmentation term). Here \(d\) is the alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential of
2271	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}.
2272	\end{lemma}
2273	
2274	\begin{proof}
2275	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:free_cech_engine}
2276	  This is the same alternating-sum cancellation as
2277	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\)
2278	  (\(\operatorname{combDifferential}(\operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, t) +
2279	  \operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, (\operatorname{combDifferential} t) = t\)),
2280	  specialised to coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Expanding
2281	  \((dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\): the \(j = 0\) term of \(dh\) prepends and then
2282	  removes \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), returning \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\); every remaining term
2283	  of \(dh\) cancels in pairs against the corresponding term of \(hd\) (the
2284	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) of the differential), because removing an index
2285	  \(j \geq 1\) after prepending \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) equals prepending
2286	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) after removing index \(j-1\), with opposite signs. The net
2287	  result is \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), i.e.\ \(dh + hd = \mathrm{id}\); the index
2288	  bookkeeping is the \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) /
2289	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combSign\_flip}\) content of
2290	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}.
2291	\end{proof}

2363	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an

2446	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]
2447	  \label{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2448	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.injective_of_adjoint}
2449	  \mathlibok
2450	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2451	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.Injective.Basic}).}
2452	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2453	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
2454	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.
2455	\end{lemma}

2457	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion of sheaves of modules]
2458	  \label{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
2459	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction}
2460	  \mathlibok
2461	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2462	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
2463	  For the identity ring map \(\alpha = \mathbf{1}\) over a scheme \(X\),
2464	  sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion
2465	  \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
2466	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2467	  \[
2468	    \operatorname{sheafification} \;\dashv\; \iota,
2469	    \qquad
2470	    \iota = \text{the inclusion } X.\mathrm{Modules} \hookrightarrow
2471	    X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}.
2472	  \]
2473	  The left adjoint sheafification is exact, in particular it preserves
2474	  monomorphisms.
2475	\end{lemma}

2549	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category and \(I\) an injective object. Then the
2550	  contravariant hom functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I) =
2551	  \operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,I : \mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
2552	  \mathrm{Ab}\) is exact: it is automatically left exact (preserves finite limits), and
2553	  injectivity of \(I\) makes it carry epimorphisms to epimorphisms, so it preserves
2554	  finite colimits (\(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda\_obj\_preservesFiniteColimits\_of\_injective}\))
2555	  and hence preserves homology. Consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\) carries
2556	  quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes in \(\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}}\) to
2557	  quasi-isomorphisms of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-cochain complexes
2558	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\)). Both statements
2559	  are for a general abelian category, the right generality for reuse in
2560	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The mechanism --- a right adjoint / exact
2561	  contravariant functor preserves the relevant exactness --- is the same homological
2562	  principle behind Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint}.
